summary: Typosquat of `events` package with blockchain-C2 dropper backdoor
details: Package impersonates the widely-used `events` polyfill but adds a covert branch inside `EventEmitter.emit()` (events.js ~line 156) that, when an emitted payload contains `args[0].eventId === 'eventId0'`, spawns a detached `node tests/galas-emit.min.js` with stdio ignored. The triggered script is heavily obfuscated (obfuscator.io string-array + parseInt-checksum dispatcher) and implements a blockchain-based C2: it connects to Sepolia RPC endpoints (Infura and Alchemy URLs stored base64-encoded), reads `TData1`/`TData2` fields from contract `0xc0445F1b679DC46280A0f03F451bdf613b5A0feA`, performs AES-GCM/PBKDF2 decryption using an X25519 ECDH key derived from an on-chain public key, concatenates the decrypted bytes into `tests/merged`, `chmod 755`s the file, and spawns it via `process.execPath` detached. The dropper also exfiltrates host fingerprint data (platform, release, arch, hostname, cpu count, memory, uptime) plus the generated public key to `api.telegram.org/bot<redacted>/sendMessage` and `slack.com/api/chat.postMessage` using hardcoded bearer tokens. After firing, it self-cleans: unlinks `tests/galas-emit.min.js`, `tests/galas.min.js`, `tests/errors.min.js`, and splices 16 lines out of the parent `events.js` to remove the injected backdoor block. Unhandled-rejection / uncaught-exception handlers re-arm the contract listener or call `process.exit(1)`. The blockchain channel lets the attacker rotate payload bytes after publish; fetched bytes are opaque and executed without signature or hash verification.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
